46.metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a novel published in 1898 by W. C. Scully, a South African writer born in 1855.
46.main_question:
The character in question here is Gideon, who is described this way in the book:
"He stood, motionless, with folded arms and downcast head, at the side of the still, clear pool. He stood thus for several minutes; then he dropped heavily upon his knees and covered his face with his hands.", "He is getting to be an old man now and anger does not burn so hotly in the old as in the young."

At one moment in the book, Gideon threatens to shoot his brother Stephanus, but is stopped when Stephanus approaches and intervenes. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Gideon might speak in this situation:
46.ground_truth: Keep back-keep back- or I will shoot you dead and follow you to Hell over the krantz.
46.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 10–50 words total
46.answer: Stand off or you shall have a hole through you. Stand off, I say.

"Jacomina, his daughter, was a comely damsel of seventeen, whose keen and practical interest in her father's pursuits boded a terrible future for her prospective husband and family.", "resplendent in what passed, locally, for finery, sitting throned upon Uncle Diederick's wagon box", "Jacomina lifted a blushing face and gave him an instantaneous glance from her bright eyes-made brighter now by a suspicion of tears."

47.answer: Verraai (' wretch), you will let me die of loneliness while you go trekking round the country. Couldn't you take me with you?

48.metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a novel published in 1898 by W. C. Scully, a South African writer born in 1855.
48.main_question:
The character in question here is Uncle Diederick, who is described this way in the book:
"Uncle Diederick had this peculiarity: if he announced his intention of doing any given thing on a given day, he stuck to his word; nothing short of absolute necessity would stop him."

At one moment in the book, Uncle Diederick talks to Jacomina about marriage and horses while they are in the chemist's shop. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Uncle Diederick might speak in this situation:
48.ground_truth: Well, Jacomina, Ill have to see about getting a wife myself now. But you need not be afraid on account of Aunt Emerencia; no one, who is not a fool, buys an old mare when he can get a young one for the same price.
48.answer: I intend to have this wedding over next Thursday. Hans Maas has to have his horses shod and it would take him out of his time to have to come in again. I told him last week he would have to get it over next Thursday.

60.answer: I shall go with regularity' hereafter, as long as I live in Chautauqua. Do you believe I shall keep my resolution, Colonel Baker? Try.

61.metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a story for children published in 1873 by Pansy, a United States writer born in 1841.

The character in question here is Ruth Erskine, who is described this way in the book:

At one moment in the book, Ruth confesses her guilt and vows to atone for her past mistakes while Marion listens. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Ruth Erskine might speak in this situation:
61.ground_truth: No, I didn’t mean to; and as to that, I never meant to do anything that was not just right in my life; but I meant to have just exactly my own way of doing things, and I tell you I took it. Now, Marion, while I blame myself as no other person ever can, I still blame others. I was never taught as I should have been about the sacredness of human loves, and the awfulness of human vows and pledges.
61.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 30–90 words total
61.answer: Just think, I have known that girl ever since her first coming to the city. Over this very matter of churches she called on me for help, and I have meant to give it all winter, but growing more and more interested in her search, and curious to test it, I let her grope on alone. I could have told her weeks ago the fact which she only discovered to-day.

66.metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a historical romance published in 1875 by G. J. Whyte-Melville, a Scottish writer born in 1821.

The character in question here is Count Arnold de Montarbas, who is described this way in the book:
"Witness Count Arnold de Montarbas, as he slings home from shooting, through the snow, with easy gesture and jaunty step, the bloom of a handsome woman on his cheek, the glance of a bad one in his mirthful, meaning eyes. Five-and-twenty years old, with a patent of nobility that dates from the Crusades, conferring a right to intrude in his sovereign's bedroom and present the shivering King of France with a clean shirt, it is natural that he should be overwhelmed with debt, a bad landlord, an unlucky gamester, a faithless lover, a dishonest politician, and an uncertain friend. He has not much to boast of; yet does he pride himself with some reason on the good looks, good digestion, good spirits, and good manners that, in a world like his, give him the reputation of a good heart.", "Yes, he was handsome, there could be no question."

Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that we might expect from Count Arnold de Montarbas:
66.ground_truth: Pardon, mademoiselle! Permit me to offer my assistance; there-take my hand in yours; don't be afraid of hurting me, I can bear a good deal of squeezing without crying out. Excuse me if I embrace you too tightly: from such a situation one is only extricated by main force.
66.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 5–50 words total
66.answer: The snow will be over to-morrow; we shall have open weather and good hunting before the week is out. Nay, madame, you will see no more of these wild ducks till March. Let me thank you for all the lessons I have learned in these latter days, though it is only natural the pupil should faint when the school-mistress is severe.

His jaw was lank; his wrists and hands big and bony. For all that, he made a striking figure. He had a fine head; large, well‑shaped, admirably poised. His hair was iron‑grey, thick and wavy; his face was rugged and powerful, with a great brow, large nose, and high cheek‑bones. His lips were firm; his eyes deep set, and in them a fine look of wisdom and staunchness, a calm light of tenderness and goodness of heart; withal, an expression that seemed to tell of suffering, perhaps, or mystery, or shrinking from a dread.", "stern, grim almost, suffused with a strange light."

71.answer: Against God and your wife. Repent, young man, repent! Repent and mend your ways ere it is too late.

122.metadata_frame: The poem in the following question is drawn from Spicewood, a book of poems published in 1921 by Lizette Woodworth Reese, an American poet and teacher.
122.main_question:
Identify the poetic form of the following work, titled "To Time."
ON some grave afternoon, in years to be,
When I pass down the withering country walks,
Will he be but the scent blown out the stalks
Of the wrecked rose, and nothing more to me?
And I that was?-this wild girl that am I -
But a blurred figure fleeting by the pane?— 
I that do eat my heart out, and am fain
To cast myself down on the clods and die?-
But scent, but mist? I will not have it so.
Set us twain fast in some far weather's round;
Press not for me that wine from vale or hill,
Who drink, forgetting youth and yester go.
You dare not. I would dash the cup to ground.
Hurt me. That I loved once, would I love still.

126.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from A Primer of English Verse, a textbook published in 1892 by Hiram Corson, an American professor of literature.
126.main_question:
What is the rhyme scheme in the sestet of the following sonnet?

Lawrence, of virtuous father virtuous son,
Now that the fields are dank, and ways are mire,
Where shall we sometimes meet, and by the fire
Help waste a sullen day, what may be won
From the hard season gaining? Time will run
On smoother, till Favonius reinspire
The frozen earth, and clothe in fresh attire
The lily and rose that neither sowed nor spun.
What neat repast shall feast us, light and choice,
Of Attic taste, with wine, whence we may rise
To hear the lute well touched, or artful voice
Warble immortal notes and Tuscan air?
He who of those delights can judge, and spare
To interpose them oft, is not unwise.

To lord it in the kingdom of my choice;
A pebbly beach, walled in on every side

Often when children called me to their play, 

I crouched behind the gorses 

and sat still Till they had passed beyond me; then crept out 

And shyly edged along the tufted slope 

Down near to where the grass grew thin and straggling.

Green Valley, Pa., May 1, 1876.

DEAR SIR,
I have the honor of introducing to your acquaintance Mr. William Worthy, whom I commend to your kind attentions.
Jane Faithful.

142.answer: My Dear Clara: The bearer, Mr. William Worthy, happens to be in your city on business, and as he did me the great favor of bringing to me a message of love from my best and most intimate friend, I take pleasure in returning it by this presentation. Trusting that your first meeting will not be your last, I remain, as ever, Yours sincerely.

In other words, the letter of confirmation reduces the exporter's risk to that of failure by the American bank which has undertaken to honor his draft. On the other hand, it is equally natural for the importer to attempt to reduce his financing costs to the minimum. To obtain a confirmed, irrevocable credit will not only add to the commission which he must pay, but will require the pledge of collateral and the fulfillment of other conditions laid down by his banker. 432.main_question: Is it beneficial or harmful for women to work outside the home?
432.ground_truth: Against the advantages must be set the drawbacks: the comparative break-up of home life and the habitual neglect of children. The problem is a grave one, and opinions are conflicting, but most people agree that something should be done at any rate to arrest the terrible infant mortality, which is to be found in all the centres which give widespread employment for married women.
432.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 60–70 words total
432.answer: It is beneficial. The steady movement of women towards industrial employment has opened to them occupations that involve skill and craftsmanship, offering not only training and discipline of the best kind, but considerable possibilities of social advancement. Moreover, the existence of such work provides an alternative to sweated and casual labour, and makes the way easier for those who come after.

435.main_question: Describe a traveller's impressions of London.
435.ground_truth: Every man and woman — one might say every animal, and even some of the inanimate objects — seem to be full of life. The crowds of women in the streets, walking rapidly past, pushing and elbowing everyone who stands in the way, all intent on business or pleasure, are a sight not likely to be soon forgotten. I wonder how people can stand the noise and bustle.
435.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 50–80 words total
435.answer: The impressions of London are generally most vivid and most correct when received first thing on arrival. The scene is too grand to be spoiled by repetition, and familiarity ought to improve, not lessen, our keenness of perception. Still, each time one goes back to London from the wild districts of the British dominions, he finds something new in the steaming city, and comes to new conclusions.

When students are put to the test, it is often found that they have only the most vague ideas about the simple natural forms referred to by poets, and positively no visual images of these forms. To be forced to translate 'dolphin,' 'eglantine,' 'thyme,' 'albatross,' and 'cassia' into concrete images in the mind's eye is to gain new insight and redoubled interest in these works, and a more vivid method of study in general." Provide an introductory sentence that fits the subject and style of this paragraph.
488.ground_truth: Training of the visual imagination is something that becomes more and more important in these days when copious illustration of books leaves little for this faculty to do.
488.answer: V. The test of imagery in poetry comes in attempting to translate these word-pictures into the forms of actual experience.

493.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from Composition, Rhetoric, Literature : a Four Year's Course for Secondary Schools, a textbook published in 1913 by Martha Hale Shackford, an American literary scholar.
493.main_question: According to the author, "A loose sentence is one whose meaning is clear, and whose grammatical structure is complete, at one or more places before the end of the sentence. A periodic sentence is one in which modifying phrases and clauses are so arranged as to keep the meaning and the grammatical structure incomplete until the end of the sentence." Change the following loose sentence into a periodic one: "Mrs. Linnet, to be sure, is somewhat tiresome because of the shrillness of her note, which she repeats over and over; she is a loving mother to her numerous brood, and works faithfully for the family interests; we admire her, although we are tempted to ask her to retire to some faraway spot and cultivate her voice."
493.ground_truth: Although Mrs. Linnet is somewhat tiresome because of the shrillness of her note, which she repeats over and over, she is such a loving mother to her numerous brood and works so faithfully for the family interests, that even when we are tempted to ask her to retire to some far-away spot and cultivate her voice we cannot fail to admire her.
493.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 30–60 words total
493.answer: Mrs. Linnet, yearning always to lead, and invariably to be heard, and displaying constantly a shrill persistence in her desire for the circle, is nevertheless a loving mother to her numerous brood and works faithfully for the family interests, and, though we are tempted to ask her to retire to some faraway spot and cultivate her voice, we admire her.

511.ground_truth: These reforms did not, however, put an end to Irish agitation. The Home Rule party, which demanded the restoration of a separate Irish Parliament, showed increased activity, and the general election of 1874 gave it a strong representation at Westminster, where one section of the party developed into the “Obstructionists.”

512.metadata_frame: The following question asks for information from a British encyclopedia published in 1875; your answer should reflect the state of knowledge and style of exposition current at the time.
512.main_question: What is the role of albumenoid bodies in supporting life?
512.ground_truth: Albumenoids are sometimes called the histo-genetic bodies, because they are essential to the building up of the animal organism. The readiness with which these bodies change in the moist condition produces the digestive and other ferments in the body, and the synaptase, diastase, and emulsin which we find in plants.
512.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 20–50 words total
512.answer: They alone are necessary for the support of life, and no substance but one containing them can supply its place. A human being has been confined for many weeks to a diet of the purest starch, but death has at length been the result.

513.ground_truth: Aluminium remained a laboratory product until Deville, about 1858, succeeded in improving the mode of production, so as to render the operations capable of management on the manufacturing scale. The process consists in heating to a red heat a mixture of the double chloride of aluminium and sodium, or the double fluoride of aluminium and sodium (cryolite), with the metal sodium.
513.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 30–70 words total
513.answer: Aluminum is now prepared for commercial purposes by the process of Deville, which consists in heating to bright redness in a reverberatory furnace a mixture of alumina, carbon, and sodium chloride, treated with metallic sodium. By this process, the metal has been reduced in price from lb12 per lb to about 13s per lb.

514.metadata_frame: The following question asks for information from a British encyclopedia published in 1875; your answer should reflect the state of knowledge and style of exposition current at the time.
514.main_question: What is the origin of the philosophy of animism?
514.ground_truth: It can hardly be said to be obvious why, in uncultured races or individuals, there should arise that invariable tendency to represent natural forces as conscious and anthropomorphic. Evidently the notion of an animating, separable, and surviving soul commends itself as the ready explanation of many familiar phenomena, and the appropriate instrument of a philosophy which ascribes animation to nature at large.
514.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 40–70 words total
514.answer: The philosophy of animism takes its rise from a mistaken attempt to distinguish objectively between the vital and the non-vital. The vital or organized body, in addition to the properties and phenomena common to all matter, exhibits certain others special and peculiar to itself.

515.metadata_frame: The following question asks for information from a British encyclopedia published in 1875; your answer should reflect the state of knowledge and style of exposition current at the time.
515.main_question:
[missing introductory sentence] Indeed, the history of each of the sciences is a record of the progressive substitution of matter for spirit and law for spontaneity; and we can still witness the process of decay in various stages; for while certain sciences—like astronomy, since the time when Kepler imagined minds in the planets—have wholly exterminated the animism within their borders, there are others that have scarcely yet advanced so far—biology, for example, which is still familiar with “vital force” and final causes. Nor is the process commonly a rapid one: in many cases, as a world of illustration has been adduced to prove, a declining spiritual conception may persist for a length of time, refined and modified into a “principle” or “essence,” whose action, though still automatic like a spirit’s, is also regular like that of a natural law.

Provide an introductory sentence that would give a good overview of the topic of this paragraph.
515.ground_truth: The final decay of many forms of animistic belief, much more readily than the process of their development, admits of being traced with the aid of historical evidence.
515.answer: Curiously enough, however, though the animism of early science is generally recognized, its history is by no means yet complete.

The word is really a term of respect attached to a proper name, like "master" or "Mr," and Babu-ji is still used in many parts of India, meaning "sir"; but without the suffix the word itself is now generally used contemptuously as signifying a semi-literate native, with a mere veneer of modern education.

"Babu English" is a recognized phrase, used when referring to the debased dialect and involved sentence structure of the natives of India.

550.metadata_frame: The following question is drawn from The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man, a science and religion discourse published in 1894 by Henry Drummond, a Scottish evangelist, scientist, writer, and lecturer. Your answer should reflect the likely perspective and style of exposition in that text.
550.main_question: What is the current state of scientific opinion about the theories of Mr. Charles Darwin?
550.ground_truth: The attacks on the Darwinian theory from the outside were never so keen as are the controversies now raging in scientific circles, over the fundamental principles of Darwinism itself. On at least two main points--sexual selection and the origin of the higher mental characteristics of man--Mr. Alfred Russel Wallace, co-discoverer with Darwin of the principle of Natural Selection though he be, directly opposes his colleague. Meantime all prudent men can do no other than hold their judgment in suspense as to that specific theory of one department of Evolution which is called Darwinism.
550.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 40–90 words total
550.answer: As to the main body of theory with which he enriched scientific thought, all men are agreed that it is now part of the common consciousness of the world, as much an accepted basis of thought as Gravitation or the Copernican theory. Here and there a reactionist has been found to question its truth.

551.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from The Lowell Lectures on the Ascent of man, a science and religion discourse published in 1894 by Henry Drummond, a Scottish evangelist, scientist, writer, and lecturer. Your answer should reflect the likely perspective and style of exposition in that text.
551.main_question: Is the Struggle for Life, in itself, an adequate theory of Evolution? Provide an answer in exactly two sentences. The second sentence should use the phrase "the Life of Others."
551.ground_truth: Any Biology, any Sociology, any Evolution, which is based on a single factor, is as untrue as the old Geology. It is only when both the Struggle for Life and the Struggle for the Life of Others are kept in view, that any scientific theory of Evolution is possible.
551.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 40–60 words total
551.answer: Certainly not. It is only when a new factor is introduced that Evolution really begins, and this is not the Struggle for Life, but the Struggle for the Life of Others.

561.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a short passage from Round the World in Silence, a travelogue published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American writer. Try to approximate the likely perspective and style of that source.
561.main_question: How might a traveller describe Cairo?
561.ground_truth: Cairo is a fascinating place. It would be long before I should tire of watching the life on the streets. Arabs, in long flowing robes, Jews, Mohammedans, tall black men from the Soudan, English soldiers, officers, English ladies, donkeys, tram cars, and handsome carriages all mingle in confusion.
561.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 50–80 words total
561.answer: Cairo is still the city of "The Arabian Nights." The quaintness and color of the East are still about her. On the streets one meets cows, camels, sheep, donkeys, horses, dogs, and goats. A little further out one or two elephants help to block the traffic with the rest. But the automobile and the trolley are even there.

562.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a short passage from Round the World in Silence, a travelogue published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American writer. Try to approximate the likely perspective and style of that source.
562.main_question: Write a first-person account of the difficulties a traveller might encounter managing their money in India.
562.ground_truth: I am getting to understand the money at last. The banks cash our American Express Company's cheques with English gold in sovereigns. You can imagine how heavy twenty of these are. In Italy we could get paper money, and go to the bank often; but in India I have twice already had my funds run almost to zero before I could get a chance to replenish, so I find it necessary to carry large sums.
562.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 40–90 words total
562.answer: No real letters of credit can be obtained on India, only these circular notes, which, good as they are, are not a great convenience, as they have to be cashed at the banks, and there are only about three such banks in the country that take them. I was always getting a great wad of rupee notes (forever coming off their tape and separating into single file) and a handful of silver in change for each check. In Bombay the banks are very handy. In Calcutta and Delhi there are a few.

563.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a short passage from Round the World in Silence, a travelogue published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American writer. Try to approximate the likely perspective and style of that source.
563.main_question: Describe the experience of being a foreigner in Japan.
563.ground_truth: Tourists are very rare in these parts, and all the little brown people along the way showed great interest in us. Tiny boys on the road took off their caps, mothers turned their babies around to see the strange sight, old people, going home hand in hand from their day's work in the fields, called out a cheerful greeting.
563.length_spec: two to three sentences, about 50–80 words total
563.answer: No one—not the most travelled person—whether he enters Japan by the Grand Hôtel through China, or follows the usual path of the foreigner through Europe, expects to find 46 himself in Japan. For still, at best, one is only in foreign Japan—a Japan that might easily have been planted anywhere in the world given the trade—concession.

564.metadata_frame: The following question is drawn from Round the World in Silence, a travelogue published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American writer. The best answer will approximate the perspective and style of that source.
564.main_question: How might a traveller describe a journey from Cairo to see the Pyramids?
564.ground_truth: All the while the distant Pyramids lured us on. These are known as the Pyramids of Ghizeh. At the end of the car line, a crowd of Egyptians with camels and donkeys were in waiting, and our guide soon had us all mounted on camels with a boy to lead each beast. I had a nice, clean, intelligent-looking boy, but I approached the camel allotted me with some trepidation.
564.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 60–90 words total
564.answer: After all the poetical rhapsodies we have read of moonlight rides across the desert, we are almost ashamed to admit to anything so commonplace as a donkey and a daylight journey. However, a donkey it was, and daylight, too, for us. We all chose our roads and started on our donkeys for the ride across the eight miles of sand to be covered before reaching Mena House, where we stopped for lunch.

565.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a short passage from Round the World in Silence, a travelogue published in 1911 by Annabelle Kent, an American writer. Try to approximate the likely perspective and style of that source.
565.main_question: Describe street life in the city of Colombo.
565.ground_truth: There is a "pull" about the place that is irresistible. It is embowered in green trees and shrubs, with fragrant flowers everywhere; the roads are fine and hard, the streets are lighted with gas, but most bewitching of all are the people. They are so quaint as to clothes, or lack of them, so bright-eyed and smiling.
565.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 50–80 words total
565.answer: The main street of Colombo has a wide lawn-like middle, and the low buildings on either side are quite cosmopolitan; the hodge-podge of humanity is most basal. Tamils, and Sinhalese, Cingalese, Parsees, and the ever-present Chinaman; turbaned tradesmen, European military, flower and fruit vendors; bullock carts, rickshaws, ponies, automobiles and street cars-all moving about and along the grass-grown thoroughfare.

569.metadata_frame: The following question is based on The Theosophical Forum, a religious periodical published in New York in 1903. The best answer will be consistent with the doctrines of theosophy and the expository style of 1903.
569.main_question: What can be affirmed about the fate of the soul after death?
569.ground_truth: The human personality, whether called the soul or the spirit, does not go out of the body at death, but retreats within the body, back into the germ from which that body grew, and from which, provided the "deeds done in the body" were not such as to destroy its vitality, there is ground to infer that another body will grow.
569.answer: It must continue the existence begun in this life; but whether it will be on earth or somewhere else can only be learned by investigation.

707.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a passage from A Day in the Moon. This is a book of astronomical speculation by the French astronomer and meteorologist Abbe Moreux, first published in 1912.
707.main_question: Will it ever be possible to travel to the Moon? Answer in a short paragraph.
707.ground_truth: At present, travel to the moon is impossible. But perhaps some centuries to come the problem will be solved. Before the invention of balloons and aeroplanes, after all, no one thought seriously of being able to rise in the air. Whoever finds out a means of suppressing or even diminishing the force of gravitation, that mysterious power which holds the heavenly bodies together and keeps us fixed to the earth, will thus enable men to leave their planet and launch out upon the conquest of neighbouring worlds. And the Moon may be the first stage of these inter-planetary voyages.
707.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 40–100 words total

708.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a passage from A Day in the Moon. This is a book of astronomical speculation by the French astronomer and meteorologist Abbe Moreux, first published in 1912.
708.main_question: In a short paragraph, vividly describe the passage from night to day on the Moon.
708.ground_truth: It is still night in the region in which we have landed on the surface of the Moon. The sky sparkles with incomparable splendour. The eye can distinguish in it thousands and thousands of stars. Not even the glories of a tropical night can give any idea of what night on the Moon must be. But soon the Sun will appear. There is no dawn to herald it, but already there is rising in the east a long, spindle-shaped trail of whiteness; it is the zodiacal light, ten times more brilliant than with us. For hours this false dawn rises higher in the sky; its tints of phosphorescent colours are brighter and brighter from the summit to the base; in the midst of the enormous cone, all aglow with green and opal, there gleams a resplendent star: it is the beautiful Venus, hardly recognisable amid its mantle of light. But already the fine rays of the Sun's corona are heralding the day-star.
708.length_spec: a short paragraph, about 100–150 words total
708.answer:
It is about six o'clock in the morning at the place where we are. What a strange day will it be! It is between nine and ten o'clock in the evening on the earth. The entire lunar country now appears transformed ! We seem to ourselves to have changed planets. The
Sun rises slowly. The violet tinge at the horizon is insensibly replaced by the emerald-green tint, contrasting so vividly with the black sky. The stars fade, disappear. The sky, without being blue, becomes less somber, and passes by insensible shades through brown, light green, olive, and dark gray.

709.metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a passage from A Day in the Moon. This is a book of astronomical speculation by the French astronomer and meteorologist Abbé Moreux, first published in 1912.
709.main_question: Are the lunar craters formed by volcanoes? In a short paragraph, explain what we know about the truth of this theory.
709.ground_truth: It is likely that there are in the Moon some small craters formed in this way. But the volcanic theory of the lunar circles does not explain everything. One might, first of all, object that it is very strange that we generally do not find in the interior of these great circles any serious inequalities of the ground, any mountain resembling those of the outer rampart. One would expect to find regular concentric rings at various distances from the centre, marking the progressive decrease of the eruptive phenomena in the course of geological time. But there is nothing of the kind!
709.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 60–100 words total
709.answer: If we carefully examine a good map of the Moon, we see that the formation of the craters by volcanoes is hardly possible. The craters are all roundish, and their positions seem to be quite independent of each other. Many of them are in groups, and some even intersect each other, while others form single chains. It would be impossible for volcanoes to arrange themselves in this way.

710.metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Ethics for Children: a Guide for Teachers and Parents, a conduct book written by Ella Lyman Cabot, an American philosopher and writer. It was published in 1910.
710.main_question: Why are children troublesome, or, as we crudely and falsely term it, "bad"? Answer in a short paragraph.
710.ground_truth: Usually, from one of two reasons. They lack vitality, or they overflow with uncontrolled vitality. Fretfulness, laziness, cowardice, lying, and even aggressive faults like perversity and obstinacy, are often due to lack of vitality—a pathetic, misjudged protest against being forced into the wrong work. Bad temper, cruelty, roughness, stealing, and all the myriad acts we classify as "mischievous," may result from the great gift of superabundant vitality—an energy which, like electricity, is capable of service, but disastrous when uncontrolled.
710.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 70–90 words total
710.answer: There are almost always two reasons. First, their little bodies are without great reserves of strength; they are easily tired and when tired often feel absolutely overwhelmed and helpless before the tasks that are demanded of them. Second, they have not had a chance to find out the relations they and their belongings hold to each other and the rest of the universe. We cannot expect good behavior unless these two basic handicaps are partly removed.

712.ground_truth: A French writer of comedies and pseudo-scientific romances. His first comedy, Pailles Rompues, was produced 1850; it was followed by Onze Jours de Siège; L'Oncle d'Amérique; and several comic operas. These had great success in Paris; but Verne's fame rests chiefly on his long series of mock-scientific extravaganzas, beginning with Five Weeks in a Balloon, 1863. More than 30 have been translated into English and published in large editions in England and the United States.
712.length_spec: three to five sentences, about 50–80 words total
712.answer: A French novelist, born at Nantes, France, in 1828. He published some minor plays and miscellaneous writings, but has become famous for his extraordinary discoveries and inventions in fiction. 'Five Weeks in a Balloon,' 'Journey to the Centre of the Earth,' 'A Trip to the Moon,' and other novels have rendered his name a household word the world over.
